---
title: "37 · Off-Policy Actor-Critic and Importance Sampling"
subtitle: "Behaviour policy gathers data; target policy is learned. Importance sampling is the bridge."
lesson-id: "mfrl.off-policy-actor-critic"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.advantage-actor-critic"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state **A2C** ([Lesson 36](../36-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd))?
2. Do you know the difference between **on-policy** and **off-policy**
   sampling?
3. Are you comfortable with **expected value** under different
   distributions, and can you write
   $\mathbb E_p[f(X)] = \int f(x) p(x)\, dx$?

If yes to all three, continue.
:::

## Touch

A widget shows three different policies — call them $\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3$ —
each evaluated using the *same* batch of historical data. The data was
generated by a single behaviour policy $\beta$ a long time ago. Each policy
is reweighted by its own importance ratios.

Click "evaluate". The widget produces three different value estimates from
the *same* underlying transitions, simply by reweighting them according to
$\pi_i(a) / \beta(a)$.

::: {.lesson-beat}
One stack of data. Three target policies. Importance sampling is the
bridge — it lets us pretend the data came from any policy we like, at the
price of variance.
:::

## Wonder

1. The data was sampled by $\beta$, not $\pi_\theta$. The policy gradient
   theorem says $\mathbb E_{(s, a) \sim \pi_\theta}[\cdots]$. How can the
   data be valid for $\pi_\theta$'s gradient?
2. If the reweighting "fixes" the distribution mismatch, why does
   off-policy still come at a cost?
3. Suppose $\pi_\theta$ and $\beta$ disagree wildly. What goes wrong with
   the importance ratios?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — the importance sampling identity

For any positive density $\beta$ that covers $\pi$ (i.e., $\beta(a) > 0$
whenever $\pi(a) > 0$):

$$\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi}[f(a)] \;=\; \mathbb E_{a \sim \beta}\!\left[\frac{\pi(a)}{\beta(a)}\,f(a)\right].$$

Verify this.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write both sides as integrals against the relevant density.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS: $\int \pi(a) f(a)\, da$.
RHS: $\int \beta(a) \frac{\pi(a)}{\beta(a)} f(a)\, da = \int \pi(a) f(a)\, da$. ✓

The factor $\pi(a)/\beta(a)$ is the **importance ratio** (sometimes
written $\rho(a) = \pi(a)/\beta(a)$). It reweights samples drawn under $\beta$
to be representative of $\pi$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — apply IS to the policy gradient theorem

The policy gradient theorem is
$\nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb E_{(s, a) \sim \pi_\theta}[\nabla \log
\pi_\theta(a \mid s) q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)]$. Suppose data is sampled by
$\beta$ (not $\pi_\theta$). Apply the IS identity at the action level:

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set $f(a) = \nabla \log \pi(a \mid s) q_\pi(s, a)$ and reweight by
$\pi_\theta(a \mid s) / \beta(a \mid s)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$\nabla J(\theta) \;\approx\; \mathbb E_{s \sim d^\beta,\ a \sim \beta(\cdot \mid s)}\!\left[\frac{\pi_\theta(a \mid s)}{\beta(a \mid s)}\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\,q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right].$$

The "$\approx$" hides one subtlety: the *state distribution* in the original
PG theorem is $d^{\pi_\theta}$, but our data has states drawn from
$d^\beta$. Strictly correcting for that requires another importance ratio
across the *whole trajectory* — variance-explosively. In practice we accept
the state-distribution mismatch and only correct at the action level. This
is **off-policy actor-critic** (Degris, White & Sutton 2012).
</details>

### Problem 3 — the variance hazard

Let the importance ratio be $\rho_t = \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) / \beta(a_t
\mid s_t)$. Suppose $\beta$ chooses $a_t$ with probability 0.01 but
$\pi_\theta$ chooses it with probability 0.5. What is $\rho_t$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\rho_t = 0.5 / 0.01 = 50$. A single sample can multiply the gradient by 50,
producing a huge estimate. Now imagine multiplying *several* such ratios
along a trajectory — say 20 such steps. $50^{20} \approx 10^{34}$.
Multi-step IS is variance-disastrous. This is why off-policy methods
either (a) only correct one step (Q-learning's max ignores the
behaviour-policy mismatch entirely) or (b) clip the ratios (PPO's clipped
ratio = 1±ε).
</details>

### Problem 4 — what off-policy AC buys

Why is off-policy worth the variance trouble?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Data efficiency. Reuse stale data.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Data efficiency.** On-policy methods discard each trajectory after one
gradient step. Off-policy methods can reuse each transition many times
(replay buffer, multi-epoch updates). On environments where simulation is
expensive (real robots, large simulators), the data efficiency win can
be enormous. The price is variance inflation, paid in compute (more
samples needed to drive variance down) and engineering (clipping,
trust-region constraints).
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Importance sampling** was developed by mathematicians — most famously
**Marshall Hall and John von Neumann** — in WWII for ballistics
calculations: simulate rare events by reweighting common ones. RL adopted
it 60 years later for the same reason: *use samples you've already paid
for*. The name "off-policy" itself was coined by Sutton, distinguishing
"the policy that *generates* the data" from "the policy that is
*evaluated*".
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Off-policy is data efficiency.** Reuse stale data. The cost is
variance, paid through importance ratios. Importance sampling is the
mathematical bridge: it reweights samples from one distribution to be
unbiased for another. Modern off-policy methods (PPO's clipping, V-trace,
Retrace) are all about controlling that variance.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Off-policy actor-critic.** Data is generated by a behaviour policy
$\beta$. The actor's update for the *target* policy $\pi_\theta$ uses an
importance-weighted policy gradient:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha\,\rho_t\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\,\hat A(s_t, a_t),
\qquad \rho_t = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\beta(a_t \mid s_t)}.$$

The critic is also typically off-policy (e.g., uses Q-learning targets or
Retrace). The state-distribution mismatch ($d^\beta \ne d^{\pi_\theta}$)
is usually ignored.

**Importance sampling identity:** for any $\beta$ that covers $\pi$,
$\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi}[f(a)] = \mathbb E_{a \sim \beta}[(\pi/\beta) f(a)]$.

:::

## Apply

### Worked example — IS-corrected gradient on a 2-state MDP

State $s_1$, two actions $\{a_1, a_2\}$. Behaviour policy $\beta(a_1) =
0.9,\ \beta(a_2) = 0.1$. Target policy (current) $\pi_\theta(a_1) = 0.4,\
\pi_\theta(a_2) = 0.6$.

A behaviour rollout takes $a = a_1$ at $s_1$, observes advantage estimate
$\hat A(s_1, a_1) = -3$ (the action was below average for $\pi$).

Importance ratio: $\rho = \pi_\theta(a_1) / \beta(a_1) = 0.4 / 0.9 \approx
0.44$.

Score-function gradient at $\theta = 0$ (with state-independent softmax,
$\pi = (0.4, 0.6)$): $\nabla \log \pi(a_1) = (1 - 0.4, -0.6) = (0.6, -0.6)$.

Off-policy actor update:
$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \cdot 0.44 \cdot (0.6, -0.6) \cdot (-3)$
$= \theta + \alpha \cdot (-0.79,\ 0.79)$.

The probability of $a_1$ falls slightly (correct: $\hat A < 0$). The IS
ratio scales the gradient *down* because $\pi$ would have chosen $a_1$ less
often than $\beta$ did.

Now consider a sample at $a = a_2$ with $\hat A(s_1, a_2) = 2$. Ratio $\rho
= 0.6 / 0.1 = 6$. The off-policy update multiplies the gradient by 6 — a
large, high-variance correction because $\beta$ rarely picked $a_2$ but
$\pi$ would.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** State the importance sampling identity.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi}[f(a)] = \mathbb E_{a \sim \beta}\!\left[(\pi/\beta) f(a)\right]$,
provided $\beta(a) > 0$ wherever $\pi(a) > 0$.
</details>

**W2.** What is the importance ratio $\rho_t$ in off-policy AC?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\rho_t = \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) / \beta(a_t \mid s_t)$ — the ratio of
target-policy probability to behaviour-policy probability for the action
that was actually taken.
</details>

**W3.** When does $\rho_t$ blow up?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
When $\beta(a_t \mid s_t)$ is small but $\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ is not.
That is, when $\beta$ rarely takes an action that $\pi_\theta$ likes. The
correction must scale up to compensate, producing high-variance gradient
estimates.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** What does **PPO's clipping trick** do, in one sentence?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
PPO clips $\rho_t$ to $[1 - \varepsilon,\ 1 + \varepsilon]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
PPO replaces $\rho_t$ in the gradient by $\text{clip}(\rho_t, 1 -
\varepsilon, 1 + \varepsilon)$, capping the magnitude of any single
sample's contribution. It is no longer an unbiased gradient — but is
*much* lower variance, and still climbs $J$ in practice. PPO is essentially
*off-policy AC with clipped IS*.
</details>

**P2.** Suppose $\pi_\theta = \beta$. What does the off-policy AC update
reduce to?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\rho_t = 1$ for all transitions, so the update is identical to A2C
(on-policy). Off-policy AC strictly contains A2C as the special case of
matched policies. The IS correction is "free" in the sense that with
matched policies it does nothing; it only kicks in when there is a
behaviour-target mismatch to correct.
</details>

**P3.** Why does Q-learning *not* need an explicit IS correction even
though it's off-policy?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Q-learning's target is $r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a')$. The $\max$
doesn't involve the behaviour policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Q-learning's target $\max_{a'} \hat q(s', a')$ is independent of how the
data was sampled — it depends on $(s, a, r, s')$ alone. So the target
itself doesn't need reweighting. The state-action distribution does
matter (for *what* you bootstrap from), but Q-learning ignores that — and
that's exactly the deadly-triad hazard from [Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd). Off-policy
*actor-critic* must correct because $\mathbb E_a[\nabla \log \pi(a) \cdot
\text{advantage}]$ is over the policy's distribution, not the behaviour's.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Multi-step IS uses the product $\prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1}
\pi(a_k)/\beta(a_k)$. Why is this *much* worse than 1-step IS, even
though it's still mathematically unbiased?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance compounds multiplicatively.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each $\rho_k$ has finite variance. Their product's variance compounds: if
$\text{Var}(\rho_k)$ is moderate, the product's variance grows
exponentially in $n$. For $n$ even moderately large (say 20 steps), the
estimator's variance is astronomical and useless in practice. This is
why **Retrace** (Munos et al. 2016) and **V-trace** (Espeholt et al.
2018, IMPALA) clip or truncate the product — biased but tractable.
</details>

**S2.** Sketch why the *state* distribution mismatch is usually ignored
in off-policy AC, even though it formally biases the gradient.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Correcting for state distribution requires multi-step trajectory IS,
which is variance-impossible.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A formally correct off-policy PG would also reweight states by
$d^\pi(s)/d^\beta(s)$ — but $d^\pi$ is the *stationary* state
distribution, requiring trajectory-length IS to estimate. The variance is
prohibitive. Practitioners accept the state-distribution bias and use
1-step action-level IS only. The off-policy PG theorem (Degris-White-Sutton
2012) shows this approximation has good local properties, even though it
isn't exact. PPO, IMPALA, ACER, MPO — all make this same approximation.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Importance sampling fixes everything."** No — it makes the
   estimator unbiased only at the *action* level, not the *state* level
   (which would need trajectory-IS, variance-impossible). And it
   massively inflates variance.
2. **"Off-policy means you can use any data."** Only with $\beta(a) > 0$
   wherever $\pi_\theta(a) > 0$ (the *coverage* condition). Without
   coverage, $\rho$ is undefined and the estimator is invalid.
3. **"PPO is on-policy."** PPO is off-policy with a clipping correction
   that controls IS-ratio variance. Each PPO mini-batch is technically
   off-policy w.r.t. the version of $\pi_\theta$ that generated it.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **Off-policy AC reweights samples by the importance ratio**
   $\rho_t = \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)/\beta(a_t \mid s_t)$. Unbiased at
   the action level; the state-distribution mismatch is tolerated.
2. **Variance is the price.** When $\beta$ and $\pi_\theta$ differ
   substantially, $\rho$ can be huge or tiny, blowing up the gradient
   estimator.
3. **Modern off-policy methods control variance by clipping**: PPO clips
   $\rho$, V-trace caps it, Retrace caps the product. The bias-variance
   trade is the central engineering knob.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the IS identity from memory?
2. Did you derive the off-policy AC update yourself in *Discover* P2?
3. Could you explain why PPO is technically off-policy and why its
   clipping is a variance-reduction trick?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 38 — DDPG](../38-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo P3 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and *Apply*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.advantage-actor-critic](../36-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd)** —
  the on-policy AC skeleton this lesson generalises.

### What this unlocks

- **[DDPG](../38-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd)** — uses
  off-policy data via a replay buffer and a deterministic policy gradient
  that *avoids* IS entirely.
- Modern on-policy methods (PPO, TRPO) — clipped importance sampling.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every modern actor-critic method is at least *moderately*
off-policy. PPO (RLHF, robotics, game-playing) uses clipped IS within a
single batch. IMPALA (DeepMind's distributed framework) uses V-trace. SAC
(maximum-entropy continuous control) uses replay buffers and IS-free
deterministic gradients in the actor. Off-policy is *the* lever for sample
efficiency at scale.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The bias-variance trade in off-policy estimators is one of
the most active research directions in RL theory: doubly-robust estimators,
weighted IS, marginalised IS, off-policy evaluation. Each tries to extract
more value from a fixed batch of data than vanilla IS allows.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §10 (off-policy actor-critic). Original references:
  Degris, White & Sutton (2012), *Off-Policy Actor-Critic*; Munos et al.
  (2016), *Safe and Efficient Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning* (Retrace);
  Espeholt et al. (2018), *IMPALA* (V-trace).
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover walks the learner from
  IS identity to off-policy PG before *Name*); Better Explained "Aha!"
  framing (the key insight that off-policy buys data efficiency at the
  price of variance).
:::
